scenario: A form needs to display the company logo at the top. Team wonders if Forms support image rendering.
question: Form image display?

options.text: Camunda 8 Forms do not support displaying images â€” this approach has limitations. The Camunda Forms schema includes an Image component type that renders a static image by URL within the form layout. The Image component accepts a URL pointing to an accessible image resource and supports configuration of alternative text. It is useful for displaying logos, product images, instructional diagrams, or any visual content as part of a form. The Image component is a display-only element and does not interact with form data or process variables.

Camunda Forms support only text content and have no visual media elements -- this approach has limitations. md:2-3) for displaying images within forms. }}, or a static value (hyperlink or base64 data URI). This enables forms that display product photos, diagrams, QR codes, or logos dynamically based on process context. The Image view is a display-only element with no data binding -- it is part of the presentation layer, not form submission data.

While the HTML view element can render images using HTML tags, it requires writing raw HTML with Mustache templating for dynamic sources. The dedicated Image view element provides the same capability with a structured property interface: Image source (FEEL/template/static URL), Alternative text (accessibility), Hide if (conditional display), and Columns (layout). md.

, =productImageUrl), Mustache-style templating {{imageVar}}, or a static URL/base64 data URI. Alternative text (:14) provides accessibility fallback text. Hide if (:15) is a FEEL boolean for conditional display (true = hidden). Columns (:16) controls Carbon-grid layout sizing. The Image view is display-only -- no Key property, not included in form submission variables. For uploaded document preview, use the separate Document preview element.

a.text: Incorrect. Form image display via Image view element: Image view element (forms-element-library-image.md:2-9; sibling of HTML view / Text view / Spacer in the pure-presentation group): Drag from palette into form (palette label = "Image view" per forms-element-library.md:124-128). Configure Image source: hyperlink, data URI, OR FEEL/templating to pull from form/variable state. Set Alternative text (a11y + fallback when image fails to load). Optional: Hide if (conditional rendering, inverted FEEL boolean), Columns (grid layout). No Key property -- image is presentation-only, not bound to a form variable. Image source value modes (per forms-element-library-image.md:13 -- three modes): Static value -- hyperlink: External image: https://example.com/logo.png. Self-hosted: serve images from app or CDN. In the property panel, type the URL directly (no = prefix). Static value -- data URI: Base64-encoded data URI: data:image/png;base64,iVBORw0KGgo.... Pros: self-contained (no external dependency); offline-friendly. Cons: form schema balloons in size; not cache-friendly. FEEL expression (dynamic from variable): Prefix with =: =customerPhotoUrl or ="https://cdn.example.com/avatars/" + userId + ".png". Used for per-instance images (e.g., customer photo from process variable). Templating syntax (Mustache-style, per forms-config-templating-syntax.md): {{baseUrl}}/logo-{{theme}}.png -- interpolates variables in a string template. Useful when only part of the URL is dynamic. Use cases: Use case 1: Branding: Company logo at top of form. Consistent visual identity. Use case 2: Product images: Show product being reviewed. Customer profile photo. Use case 3: Visual instructions: Diagrams explaining what to do. Reference images. Limitations: No interactive images: just display. No zoom / pan: typically static. Size constraints: depend on layout. For document attachments (different): Document Handling (per Set 14 Q14): separate concept. PDFs, attachments: rendered via Document component. Inline thumbnails: may or may not be supported. Best practices: Use CDN for images: fast loading, cached. Optimise size: don't bloat forms with huge images. Alt text: accessibility. Test rendering: across browsers, devices. For dynamic content: Verify component supports variable-bound URL. May need custom Tasklist for advanced cases. For custom layouts: HTML component (Q32): full HTML control. Use sparingly; native components preferred.
